title: Cat Blood Inside the Eye (Hyphema): Causes and Emergency Triage
meta_desc: Blood in your cat's eye (hyphema) is a high-priority emergency. Learn why it happens and why you must seek immediate veterinary care.
content_html:
What is Blood Inside a Cat's Eye?

Blood inside the eye, medically known as hyphema, occurs when blood collects in the anterior chamber of the eye—the space between the clear cornea and the colored iris. You may see a small pool of red at the bottom of the eye, or in severe cases, the entire eye may appear dark red or cloudy. This is not a disease itself, but a sign of a significant underlying health problem or injury.

Is it an Emergency?

Yes. The urgency level for blood inside the eye is high. Hyphema is a medical emergency because it is often painful and can lead to permanent blindness or secondary glaucoma if not treated immediately. Furthermore, it is frequently a secondary symptom of life-threatening conditions such as severe high blood pressure, internal trauma, or clotting disorders. You should contact an emergency veterinarian right away.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Capturing a clear, well-lit photograph of your cat's eye can be an invaluable tool for the veterinary triage team. A photo allows the doctor to assess the volume of blood and the condition of the surrounding structures before you even arrive at the clinic. It also provides a baseline for monitoring whether the bleeding is worsening during your commute to the animal hospital.

faqs.question: Can hyphema go away on its own?
faqs.answer: While the blood may eventually be reabsorbed, the underlying cause will not resolve without treatment. Failure to treat the root cause can lead to permanent vision loss or the loss of the eye itself.

faqs.question: What are the most common causes of blood in a cat's eye?
faqs.answer: Common causes include blunt force trauma, systemic hypertension (high blood pressure), uveitis (inflammation), eye tumors, or exposure to toxins like rat poison which affects blood clotting.

faqs.question: Will my cat be in pain?
faqs.answer: Yes, blood in the eye is typically associated with increased intraocular pressure or inflammation, both of which are painful for cats. Your cat may squint, paw at the eye, or hide.
